topic: Unit 1: Writing for Purpose and Audience and Reading to Access Non-fiction and Media Texts
subject: english-language

items.q: Why do text types like a letter, report or advert have predictable features?
items.correct: Because their conventions help readers recognise the type and grasp the purpose quickly.
items.misconception: Because writers are forced to copy a fixed template for no real reason.
items.random_wrong: Because all texts are written by the same author.
items.why: Students see conventions as arbitrary rules rather than features that aid communication.

items.q: What is the difference between skimming and scanning a text?
items.correct: Skimming is reading quickly for the general gist; scanning is searching for specific information.
items.misconception: They are two words for the same reading technique.
items.random_wrong: Skimming means reading every word very slowly.
items.why: Learners blur the two reading strategies that serve different purposes.

items.q: When writing for a specific audience, what should most influence your choices?
items.correct: The audience's age, knowledge and expectations, which shape vocabulary, tone and content.
items.misconception: Only the topic matters; the audience makes no difference.
items.random_wrong: You should use the hardest possible words for every audience.
items.why: Students neglect audience as a key factor shaping appropriate writing.

items.q: What does the L in PEEL stand for?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Link. “List” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which reading skill hunts specific facts?
items.why: It's Scanning. “Skimming” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many letters in PEEL paragraph structure?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 letters. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
